note: Small, deterministic fallbacks for connecting a current story to official numbers. A reviewed entry in home-editorial.json always takes precedence.

rules.text: If the trade talks get harder, the peso will probably react before the monthly export data does. A weaker peso would be the first signal. The next export report will show whether the disagreement is affecting actual trade.
If inflation keeps falling and Banxico holds its rate, Mexico's interest-rate advantage grows. That tends to support the peso. A renewed rise in inflation would complicate that.

Announcements are easy. The test is whether factories open, exports rise, and economic activity improves in later releases. If those numbers stay flat, the investment has not spread through the economy yet.
If tighter US enforcement is affecting Mexican workers, remittances should eventually fall. If remittances hold up, that would suggest the effect is still limited.
